metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the false premise: `--repair` (with `--dry-run`, `--limit=N`, `--repair-snapshots`) already exists on `rebar fsck`; reconcile whether WS3 introduces `--fix` as an alias/replacement for `--repair`, extends it, or is meant to be a distinct 'run_ensures' trigger — and update AC1's 'only mutation-enabling flag' claim accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan's central premise — 'today `rebar fsck` exposes ONLY `[--output json]` — there is no existing `--repair`/`--fix` (verified: tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out and src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt)' — is directly refuted by the files it names. Both `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt` line 1 and `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` line 1 read: `Usage: rebar fsck [--output json] [--repair [--dry-run] [--limit=N]]`, and `--repair`, `--dry-run`, `--limit=N`, and `--repair-snapshots` are all fully documented and implemented in `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:682`. The plan's own cited evidence refutes the claim.
findings.location: What section: 'NOTE: today rebar fsck exposes ONLY [--output json] — there is no existing --repair/--fix (verified: tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out and src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'NOTE: today rebar fsck exposes ONLY [--output json] — there is no existing --repair/--fix' with an accurate description: 'rebar fsck already has --repair (A3 remediation) and --repair-snapshots; --fix is a NEW, DISTINCT flag that invokes run_ensures() rather than the existing A3 repair path'. Update AC1 to reflect that --repair also enables mutation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's false 'no --repair flag exists' assertion: document the relationship between the new `--fix` flag (runs `run_ensures()`) and the existing `--repair` flag (A3 remediation), and confirm they are genuinely non-colliding in purpose.
findings.finding: The plan's central factual premise is wrong: it states 'today `rebar fsck` exposes ONLY `[--output json]` — there is no existing `--repair`/`--fix` (verified: tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out and src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt)', but BOTH cited files show `--repair`, `--dry-run`, `--limit=N`, and `--repair-snapshots` already exist. Adding `--fix` as a second mutation-enabling flag alongside `--repair` requires the plan to explain the distinction or rename/unify them, not assert no flag exists.
findings.location: What section — 'NOTE: today rebar fsck exposes ONLY [--output json] — there is no existing --repair/--fix (verified: tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out and src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Relationship to --repair' note in the What section: '--repair drives A3 store-integrity remediation (ticket-level fsck-zero); --fix runs the ensures registry (store-setup convergence). They are orthogonal and may both be present in a future invocation, but this WS adds only --fix.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the false 'no existing --repair' premise: `--repair` IS implemented in `fsck.py` and documented in `fsck.txt`/`fsck__help.out`; clarify how `--fix` (ensures-layer) relates to `--repair` (A3-remediation) and whether they compose, are mutually exclusive, or are merged.
findings.finding: The plan's What section contains a materially false counter-assertion: it states 'today rebar fsck exposes ONLY [--output json] — there is no existing --repair/--fix (verified: tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out and src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt)'. The actual files show the opposite: `--repair` (with `--dry-run`, `--limit=N`, `--repair-snapshots`) is fully documented in both golden files and fully implemented in `fsck.py` (lines 677–742). An implementer trusting this plan will not account for the pre-existing `--repair` flag and its interaction with the new `--fix` flag.
findings.location: What section — '...there is no existing --repair/--fix (verified: tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out and src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the false verification claim with: 'NOTE: --repair (A3 store-level remediation) already exists; --fix is a separate additive flag for the ensures-layer specifically. The plan must specify whether these flags are mutually exclusive, composable, or whether --fix should instead be a sub-option of --repair.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the pre-existing `--repair` / `--repair-snapshots` / `--dry-run` flags on `rebar fsck` (confirmed in `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt` and `fsck.py:681–695`) and define the relationship between `--fix` (`run_ensures()`) and `--repair` (`_repair_run()`): are they composable, independent, or should `--fix` be placed on a different entry point to avoid a two-mutating-flag CLI?
findings.finding: The plan falsely asserts `rebar fsck` has no existing `--repair`/`--fix` flag, when `--repair` (and `--repair-snapshots`, `--dry-run`, `--limit`) already exists at `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:681–695` and is documented in `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt`. The plan uses this false premise to declare `--fix` a 'clean ADDITIVE flag (no collision/rename)', but never addresses how two distinct mutating modes (`--repair` running `_repair_run()` vs `--fix` running `run_ensures()`) coexist on the same command, whether they overlap or compose, or whether the existing `--repair` machinery (git tagging, batched commit+push, `no_mutate` gating, dry-run) already partially satisfies the `--fix` use case.
findings.location: What section: '`rebar fsck` today exposes ONLY `[--output json]` — there is NO existing `--repair`/`--fix` (verified: tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out and src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's 'no existing --repair/--fix' premise (it is contradicted by fsck.py, fsck.txt, and the golden files) and explicitly differentiate `--fix` (runs `run_ensures()`) from `--repair` (runs `_repair_run()`) so the CLI help text, golden update scope, and AC4 byte-parity expectations are accurate.
findings.finding: The plan's foundational premise — 'today `rebar fsck` exposes ONLY `[--output json]` — there is no existing `--repair`/`--fix`' — is directly contradicted by the codebase. `--repair`, `--repair-snapshots`, `--dry-run`, and `--limit=N` are all present in `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py` (lines 681-696), `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt`, `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out`, and `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__dashhelp.out`. The 'no collision/rename' justification for `--fix` is therefore unsound, and `--fix` (running `run_ensures()`) must be rationalized against the already-present `--repair` (running `_repair_run()`) flag.
findings.location: What section: 'NOTE: today `rebar fsck` exposes ONLY `[--output json]` — there is no existing `--repair`/`--fix` (verified: tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out and src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the NOTE in 'What' with: 'NOTE: `rebar fsck` already has `--repair` (drives A3 store-to-fsck-zero via `_repair_run()`), `--repair-snapshots`, `--dry-run`, and `--limit=N`. The new `--fix` flag is additive and distinct — it runs `run_ensures()` (WS1 ensure-registry convergence) and is the ONLY flag enabling that mutation path. The help text, golden files, and overview line must be updated to reflect both repair paths.' Update AC4 to name all affected goldens explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the false factual assertion: `--repair` (and `--repair-snapshots`) already exist in `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt` and `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out`; reconcile whether `--fix` is a NEW name for a different concern or a rename/alias, and explicitly specify how `--fix` relates to the pre-existing `--repair` flag.
findings.finding: The plan's central premise — 'there is NO existing --repair/--fix flag (verified: tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out and src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt)' — is factually false. Both files plainly document --repair, --dry-run, --limit=N, and --repair-snapshots as existing mutating flags on rebar fsck. An executor following this plan will add --fix as a 'clean additive flag with no collision' while in reality --repair already occupies the mutation slot, making the collision/interaction relationship between --fix and --repair completely unaddressed.
findings.location: What section: 'NOTE: today rebar fsck exposes ONLY [--output json] — there is no existing --repair/--fix (verified: tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out and src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the 'no existing --repair/--fix' claim with the accurate statement: '--repair already exists for A3 store-remediation; --fix is a DIFFERENT flag targeting run_ensures() (WS1 ensure-units), not the A3 repair loop. Document the distinction explicitly in the What section and in the updated fsck.txt help text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'no existing --repair/--fix' assertion (both flags exist: `--repair` at `fsck.py:682` and `fsck.txt:6`), and explicitly document the intended scope boundary between `--repair` (A3 ticket-fault remediation) and `--fix` (ensures convergence) so help text, error messages, and --dry-run interaction are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan claims '`rebar fsck` today exposes ONLY `[--output json]` — there is no existing `--repair`/`--fix`' but `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt` line 1 and `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` line 1 both show `[--repair [--dry-run] [--limit=N]]` with `--repair-snapshots` also present and implemented at `fsck.py:682`. Adding a second mutation flag `--fix` (for ensures) alongside the existing `--repair` (for A3 ticket faults) is not inherently wrong, but the plan's false premise that there is 'no collision' obscures a real coexistence design decision: the plan never explains the intended relationship between the two mutation flags, leaving implementers to guess whether `--fix` and `--repair` should compose, conflict, or be documented as orthogonal.
findings.location: What section: 'NOTE: today `rebar fsck` exposes ONLY `[--output json]` — there is no existing `--repair`/`--fix` (verified: tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out and src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What/Scope sections, replace the false premise with: '`--repair` already exists for A3 ticket-fault remediation; `--fix` is additive and orthogonal (targets ensures convergence, not ticket-event faults). Update fsck.txt and the help golden to list both flags with distinct one-line descriptions.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the WS1 dependency: confirm `run_ensures()` exists (or will exist at a named module path) before WS3 can be implemented, and add a guard or import path to the plan so an implementer knows exactly where to import it from.
findings.finding: The plan invokes `run_ensures()` (described as a WS1 deliverable) in both `fsck --fix` and `mcp_server.main` startup, but `run_ensures` does not exist anywhere in `src/rebar/` — zero matches. The plan provides no guard, conditional import, or fallback for the case where WS1 has not yet landed, making the entire WS3 implementation unexecutable if WS1 is incomplete.
findings.location: What section: 'fsck --fix runs run_ensures() (WS1) under the write lock'; AC2: 'rebar fsck --fix runs run_ensures()'; AC3: 'MCP server startup runs run_ensures()'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the import path and call signature for `run_ensures()` that WS3 will use (e.g., `from rebar._ensures import run_ensures`), or add an explicit sequencing constraint that WS3 cannot merge until WS1's `run_ensures()` is available.
findings.finding: `run_ensures()` does not exist anywhere in `src/rebar/` — it is a symbol to be delivered by WS1, not yet present. The plan calls it unconditionally from `fsck --fix` and `mcp_server.main` with no guarded import, stub contract, or interface definition that WS3 can code against before WS1 lands.
findings.location: What section — 'fsck --fix runs run_ensures() (WS1) under the write lock and heals. Wire MCP server startup (mcp_server.main, after the --help check) to run run_ensures().'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the expected import path of `run_ensures()` (e.g., `from rebar.ensures import run_ensures`) and add an explicit WS1-must-merge-first landing-order constraint (or a `NotImplementedError` stub guard) so WS3 is not accidentally shipped before its dependency.
findings.finding: `run_ensures()` — the callable both `rebar fsck --fix` (AC2) and `mcp_server.main` startup (AC3) depend on — has zero matches in `src/rebar/`. It is a WS1 deliverable not yet present. The plan references it as an existing callable with no guard, import path, or explicit landing-order constraint stated in-plan.
findings.location: What section: 'fsck --fix runs run_ensures() (WS1) under the write lock'; AC2: 'rebar fsck --fix runs run_ensures()'; AC3: 'MCP server startup runs run_ensures()'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the contradiction between 'opt-in healing / --fix is the only mutation-enabling flag' (Why, AC1) and 'server startup unconditionally runs run_ensures()' (AC3) — either (a) explicitly carve out server-startup as a separate, justified mutation surface distinct from the CLI opt-in model, or (b) qualify AC3 to clarify whether run_ensures() here is read-only or write-gated.
findings.finding: AC1 ('--fix is the only mutation-enabling flag') directly contradicts AC3 ('MCP server startup runs run_ensures()'), and the Why section ('healing is opt-in') contradicts the What section (server boot auto-runs ensures without any --fix flag). An implementer would reasonably conclude either that server startup must also gate on a flag, or that the 'only mutation-enabling flag' claim is wrong.
findings.location: Why section ('healing is opt-in'); What section ('--fix is the ONLY thing that enables mutation'); AC1 ('--fix is the only mutation-enabling flag'); AC3 ('a booted server is converged')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clarifying sentence in the What section: 'Server-startup wiring calls run_ensures() as a separate mutation surface (server boot implicitly opts-in); the --fix flag is the only CLI-level mutation trigger. The no_mutate constraint applies to library surfaces, not server boot.' Then update AC1 to read '...--fix is the only CLI mutation-enabling flag' so it no longer conflicts with AC3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the AC1/AC3/Why contradiction: either (a) acknowledge that server-startup `run_ensures()` IS an always-on mutation (and update the 'opt-in' framing in Why), or (b) gate the startup call behind a config flag or the existing `REBAR_MCP_READONLY` env var, and update AC1 to name both mutation paths.
findings.finding: AC1 states '--fix is the only mutation-enabling flag' but AC3 states 'MCP server startup runs run_ensures()' unconditionally (not gated by any flag). The Why section states 'healing is opt-in until the doctor epic lands.' These three statements are mutually contradictory. No positive rationale is given for why server startup bypasses the opt-in principle that the rest of the plan enforces.
findings.location: AC1 ('--fix is the only mutation-enabling flag'), AC3 ('a booted server is converged'), Why section ('healing is opt-in')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_lib_reads.py` (which calls `fsck_cli()` and surfaces stdout to library consumers) as a consumer affected by the new `ensures: N/M applied` output line, and confirm whether its callers and tests need updating.
findings.finding: `rebar._lib_reads` (outside `_commands/` and `_cli/`) calls `fsck_cli([], ...)` and captures its stdout as a string; adding an `ensures: N/M applied` line to plain-fsck output changes what `_lib_reads.fsck()` returns to library callers, but this consumer is not enumerated in the plan's scope or edit set.
findings.location: Scope section — In: '--fix flag + ensures: N/M report line ... Out: behavioral non-fsck tests (WS5)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the return contract of `run_ensures()` as consumed by WS3: define what N and M represent (e.g. N = units whose state changed, M = total registered units) and what Python type/structure carries these values back to the fsck report formatter.
findings.finding: The `run_ensures()` return contract — specifically the interface that produces the N and M values for the `ensures: N/M applied` report line — is never defined in this plan. The plan says fsck calls run_ensures() and prints 'ensures: N/M applied' but does not specify what run_ensures() returns, what N represents (units applied this run? total registered?), what M represents (total registered? total attempted?), or what type/structure carries these values.
findings.location: What section: 'print an ensures: N/M applied line'; AC1: 'prints an ensures: N/M applied line'; Scope: 'run_ensures() (WS1)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add non-happy-path specs for `fsck --fix`: define exit code and user-visible message when `run_ensures()` partially fails or the write lock is unavailable; define MCP server behavior (abort vs. warn-and-continue) when server-startup `run_ensures()` throws.
findings.finding: The plan covers only the happy path for `rebar fsck --fix` and MCP server-startup `run_ensures()`. Error states — lock contention, partial ensures failure (some ensures succeed, some fail), and MCP server behavior when `run_ensures()` throws — are not specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria lines 1–3; What section describing `fsck --fix` and MCP wiring

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the locking/concurrency contract for MCP server-startup `run_ensures()`: does it acquire the same write lock as `fsck --fix`, and what is the behavior (block, skip, error) when the lock is already held?
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `fsck --fix` runs `run_ensures()` 'under the write lock' but the MCP server-startup call to `run_ensures()` has no locking specification. If an MCP server starts concurrently with a `rebar fsck --fix` invocation (or two servers start simultaneously), there is no defined ownership of the write lock for the server-startup path, creating a potential TOCTOU race on the shared store state.
findings.location: What section: 'Wire MCP server startup … to run `run_ensures()`' and AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly that `mcp_server.main` acquires the write lock before calling `run_ensures()`, with the same lock semantics as `fsck --fix`; document that if the lock is unavailable the server logs a warning and continues (or retries with a timeout).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism for the read-only `ensures: N/M applied` line: state explicitly whether it reads the `.ensure-applied` marker file (N = applied set size, M = registry length) or calls `run_ensures()` in a dry/no_mutate mode, and confirm this does not trigger any write.
findings.finding: The read-only `rebar fsck` (no flags) is required by AC1 to print an `ensures: N/M applied` line, but the plan never specifies the mechanism by which it derives N and M without calling `run_ensures()`. The `_scan()` path has no awareness of the ensures registry or `.ensure-applied` marker. An implementer has no specified data-flow for this read path.
findings.location: AC1 — 'rebar fsck (no flag) mutates nothing and prints an `ensures: N/M applied` line'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism for plain `rebar fsck` to derive N and M without calling `run_ensures()`: name the artifact to read (e.g. the `.ensure-applied` marker from WS2), its location, and the fallback value when it is absent.
findings.finding: Plain `rebar fsck` (no --fix flag, read-only) is specified to print `ensures: N/M applied` without calling `run_ensures()`. The plan never specifies the mechanism by which the read-only fsck path determines N and M — it must read the applied-set marker (WS2), but no such mechanism is described: which file is read, what format, what fallback if absent.
findings.location: AC1: 'rebar fsck (no flag) mutates nothing and prints an ensures: N/M applied line'; What section: 'Default fsck stays read-only and REPORTS an ensures: N/M applied line (honors no_mutate)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract for run_ensures() at MCP server startup: define whether failures are fatal (fail-fast with clear error), logged-and-continued (degraded boot), or retried — and ensure the chosen behavior is tested.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify error-handling behavior when run_ensures() raises at MCP server startup: it is unspecified whether the server should fail-fast, log-and-continue, or surface a degraded mode, creating a silent swallow or hard crash risk.
findings.location: AC3: 'MCP server startup runs run_ensures() after the --help check (a booted server is converged)'
findings.suggested_fix: Recommend: catch exceptions from run_ensures() at startup, log a structured warning (including the exception), and allow the server to boot in degraded mode (ensures skipped) rather than crashing — or explicitly choose fail-fast and document that as the policy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback specification for `run_ensures()` failure during MCP server startup: state whether a failure should abort the server, log-and-continue, or surface as a startup warning, so AC3's convergence guarantee is bounded and implementable.
findings.finding: AC3 states 'a booted server is converged' by having MCP server startup call `run_ensures()`, but no fallback is specified for the case where `run_ensures()` raises an exception or partially fails during server boot. The existing `main()` has no error-handling around `build_server().run()` (confirmed at mcp_server.py:296), and the plan adds no guidance on whether a `run_ensures()` failure should abort the server, log-and-continue, or surface as a warning.
findings.location: AC3: 'MCP server startup runs run_ensures() after the --help check (a booted server is converged)'; What section: 'Wire MCP server startup (mcp_server.main, after the --help check) to run run_ensures()'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one failure/error-path AC for `rebar fsck --fix` (e.g., behavior when `run_ensures()` raises, when the write lock is unavailable, or when ensures partially fail) to ensure the new mutation command handles errors visibly and safely.
findings.finding: No failure-path or error coverage is specified for `rebar fsck --fix`. The plan describes only the happy-path (converged store, idempotency on already-converged store) with no mention of what happens when `run_ensures()` fails, the write lock is unavailable, or the store is in a partially-broken state.
findings.location: AC bullet 2 ('rebar fsck --fix runs run_ensures() and is git-level idempotent')

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC3 a concrete in-WS3 verifiable check — either a log line emitted by run_ensures() on startup (assertable with `grep`) or a unit/integration test in the WS3 test suite that boots mcp_server.main and asserts run_ensures was invoked.
findings.finding: AC3 (MCP server startup wiring) introduces a new code path — run_ensures() called after the --help check — but specifies no observable evidence that the call actually executes on a live server boot, leaving the cutover unverified end-to-end.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'MCP server startup runs run_ensures() after the --help check (a booted server is converged)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the semantics of N and M explicitly in the plan (e.g., 'N = number of ensures that mutated state, M = total ensures evaluated') and confirm this matches the return shape run_ensures() exposes from WS1.
findings.finding: The 'ensures: N/M applied' report line leaves N and M semantically undefined — an executor must guess what to count as N (applied/healed ensures) and M (total ensures checked), risking inconsistent output and mismatched golden files.
findings.location: AC1: 'prints an ensures: N/M applied line'; What section: 'REPORTS an ensures: N/M applied line'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'The report line format is `ensures: N/M applied` where N = count of ensures that wrote/mutated state and M = total ensures evaluated; this matches the (applied, total) tuple returned by run_ensures().'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test plan entry (e.g., an integration/smoke test or explicit AC assertion with a verification method) for the MCP server-startup wiring that confirms `run_ensures()` is invoked after the --help check on a real (or realistic in-process) server boot.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no test for the MCP server-startup wiring. AC3 states 'a booted server is converged' but no test artifact, test type, or verification approach is named for this new default behavior.
findings.location: AC bullet 3 ('MCP server startup runs run_ensures() after the --help check')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC sub-item: 'A test (integration or unit with real mcp_server.main code path) verifies that run_ensures() is called during startup; the test must not bypass mcp_server.main via a stub that short-circuits the startup sequence.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the rollback/back-out posture for the fsck output format change (e.g., confirm the output change is intentionally breaking with no compat shim, or add a note that a --no-report flag or versioned output is not warranted and state why).
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges that plain 'rebar fsck' output will gain a new 'ensures: N/M applied' line (AC4: 'output changes only by the new report line') but provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequence for downstream consumers (scripts, MCP clients, CI pipelines) that parse fsck output byte-for-byte.
findings.location: AC4 ('a converged store's plain-fsck output changes only by the new report line'); What section (byte-parity tests updated)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a locking specification to the MCP server-startup `run_ensures()` call: it must acquire the same write lock as `fsck --fix`, or explicitly justify why it is safe without one (e.g. run_ensures() is internally self-locking).
findings.finding: The MCP server-startup `run_ensures()` call (AC3) has no concurrency/locking specification, while the `fsck --fix` call is specified to run 'under the write lock'. If a server boots while a `rebar fsck --fix` is in flight (or two servers boot simultaneously), the ensures write path races with no protection. The established codebase pattern is `lock.acquire(tracker, timeout=30, attempts=2, dual_window=True)`.
findings.location: AC3 — 'MCP server startup runs run_ensures() after the --help check'; What section — 'fsck --fix runs run_ensures() (WS1) under the write lock'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md or ADR entry documenting the decision that MCP server startup guarantees convergence via run_ensures(), including the rationale and the implication for startup latency and coupling.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel boot-time convergence contract (MCP server startup unconditionally runs run_ensures()) but does not call for an ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update capturing this decision, leaving future maintainers without rationale for why the server guarantees convergence at boot.
findings.location: What section: 'Wire MCP server startup (mcp_server.main, after the --help check) to run run_ensures()'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief entry to CLAUDE.md (or an ADR): 'mcp_server.main runs run_ensures() at boot (after --help check) so that a booted server is always in a converged state. Trade-off: startup may take slightly longer; errors in run_ensures() must not silently swallow or block the server — see error-handling policy below.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether MCP server-startup wiring (AC3, server-operator concern) warrants its own thin ticket separate from the CLI `--fix` flag (AC1–2, developer/CLI concern), given each is independently valuable and testable after WS1.
findings.finding: The MCP server-startup wiring is a distinct concern (server-operator / daemon-reliability persona) from the CLI `--fix` flag (developer / CLI UX persona). Each is independently valuable and releasable after WS1, so bundling them in one leaf mixes two reasons-to-change.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope / AC bullet 3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for every AC (e.g., `rebar fsck | grep -E 'ensures: [0-9]+/[0-9]+ applied'` for AC1; `git log --oneline` count before/after for AC2; a log-line assertion for AC3; `diff <golden> <actual>` for AC4).
findings.finding: No concrete proving command is specified for any of the four ACs — an executor cannot produce evidence of completion without inventing verification steps ad-hoc.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four bullets)
findings.suggested_fix:
Append a '**Proving commands**' subsection:
- AC1: `rebar fsck | grep -cE 'ensures: [0-9]+/[0-9]+ applied'` returns 1; `git diff HEAD` is empty.
- AC2: `git rev-list --count HEAD` identical before and after two `rebar fsck --fix` runs on a converged store.
- AC3: `python -m rebar.mcp_server --help` exits 0; starting the server with `--` and checking stderr/stdout for a log line emitted by run_ensures (or a test that mocks run_ensures and asserts call count ≥ 1).
- AC4: `diff tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out <(rebar fsck --help)` exits 0 after golden update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a structured log statement (at WARNING level or above) when run_ensures() fails or produces errors in both the server-startup path and the fsck --fix path, so failures are observable without manual inspection.
findings.finding: The plan adds no observability (log line, metric, or alert) for run_ensures() failures at server startup or for fsck --fix errors, leaving operators blind to convergence failures in production.
findings.location: What section / AC3 and AC2
findings.suggested_fix: In both call sites, wrap run_ensures() and emit a structured log: logger.warning('run_ensures failed: %s', exc) for exceptions, and logger.info('ensures: %d/%d applied', applied, total) for successful runs.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
